words: This conversation with Edward Swenson took place at his daughter's home on Ameri
can Ridge, August 10, 1976. The interviewer is SAM SCHRAGER.

speaker: SS
words: raised 'em.

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. Well, we had a little variety of that over there. There was one

speaker: ES
words: family that was very strict with their children, and partial. This man

speaker: ES
words: had married a woman that— no, wait a minute— This man had two children,

speaker: ES
words: a boy and a girl, when he married the second time. Then they had child

speaker: ES
words: ren together. And these two, this boy and a girl, they were not treated

speaker: ES
words: as good as the rest of the children.

speaker: SS
words: The first family?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. I can't understand why- well that man was a little bit radical in

speaker: ES
words: his ways, and his wife was pretty strict, and he took her side against

speaker: ES
words: his own children, you might say. Because he seemed to think more of the

speaker: ES
words: children he had with his second wife than those two first ones.

speaker: SS
words: Had he and his first wife gotten along pretty good when they were together?

speaker: ES
words: Well, we don't know, because he had remarried this second time before we

speaker: ES
words: came to Idaho. He was there ahead of us. And our in-laws, my oldest

speaker: ES
words: brother married into the family, and this Helrude family was marh

speaker: ES
words: ried into this other just a little. One of the Helrude men married a

speaker: ES
words: sister to the wife of this man that was so strict. And they were neigh

speaker: ES
words: bors.

speaker: SS
words: A sister to his second wife?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: How did he take out his being partial? How did he show that?

speaker: ES
words: Well, he— Now this boy that he had was the oldest of the family. Well,

speaker: ES
words: his two children were older than the children he had with this second

speaker: ES
words: wife, you know, and so that there was no other boy to show partiality to.

speaker: ES
words: Because after he married the second time they had one, two- they had

speaker: ES
words: three girls before they had a boy. So far as I know he lives in Clarkston now; that

speaker: ES
words: boy. So that his own boy was half grown up before he had

speaker: ES
words: a brother. And so he had to carry the brunt of the old man's vengenance

speaker: ES
words: when he got mad at him, you know, and he was alone, there was no other

speaker: ES
words: boys. And the way he talked to him. That oldest boyyfwas the same age

speaker: ES
words: as I was, just a few months difference in our ages, so we were together

speaker: ES
words: a lot, you know. And I can remember one day, they had a well down in the

speaker: ES
words: lower- kind of a creek run through, you know, and it was a little down

speaker: ES
words: hill to that, and they had their well there and they had a wellwheel

speaker: ES
words: up on it and run the bucket down, you know and pull the water up.

speaker: ES
words: And this boy and his sister, his full sister, they had to carry water

speaker: ES
words: from there up to the house. They had a one-by-four about six feet long

speaker: ES
words: and they would run that through the handle of two five gallon coal oil

speaker: ES
words: cans and they'd get that on their shoulders, see, and carry it up to the

speaker: ES
words: house. And one day this boy had forgotten to replace this bucket down

speaker: ES
words: at the well, so when his father- his father and my father were working;

speaker: ES
words: my father was helping him- and he came to the well and the bucket wasn't

speaker: ES
words: there, and he turned around and he says to that boy, he says, "If you

speaker: ES
words: ever leave that bucket away from here again," he says, "I'm gonna whip

speaker: ES
words: you till the blood runs down your back." That was the language he^u se .

speaker: ES
words: I got so mad at that man;I was just a kid, but from then on I hated that

speaker: ES
words: man. And one day the stepmother to this boy; I don't know what was wrong

speaker: ES
words: there at the house, but she picked up a stove poker and she just split

speaker: ES
words: his skin wide open on his head. My older brother and I, we were down

speaker: ES
words: there and he had his hat on and he says, "Come along." We went down to

speaker: ES
words: the barn, "I want to show you something." And he took his hat off and

speaker: ES
words: there was blood all in his hair there, you know and the scalp open.

speaker: ES
words: That's the way they treated him. And this man tried to run the neighbor

speaker: ES
words: hood too, but he went stuck there. He carried a long barreled six-shooter

speaker: ES
words: on his hip there the first few years, but he never had a chance to use

speaker: ES
words: it. One day he drew it and his brother-in-law took it away from him!

speaker: ES
words: And after that, why, he kind of quieted down.

speaker: SS
words: This friend of yours, who got bad treatment; was he expected to do an

speaker: SS
words: awful lot of work around the house, too?

speaker: ES
words: Well, of course, he did. They had several milk cows in those times and

speaker: ES
words: this boy and his sister, they had to help with the milking, and they kept

speaker: ES
words: a lot of hogs and they had to carry milk and stuff to the hogs, you know

speaker: ES
words: and the boy had to- the cows would graze around on the valley there, you

speaker: ES
words: know and he had to gather them up at night. Rain or shine. And their

speaker: ES
words: first house burned to the ground when the boy was— I can tell you fairly

speaker: ES
words: close, because we had moved into our new house and that was in 1901, so

speaker: ES
words: I was about sixteen or seventeen years old and that boy would be the same,

speaker: ES
words: Well, he got a bed; some blankets and stuff in the loft over the wood

speaker: ES
words: shed that he slept in, so he didn't sleep in the house.

speaker: SS
words: In the new house?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. In their new house. And they had two bedrooms upstairs and one

speaker: ES
words: bedroom downstairs, so there would have been room, but the girls they

speaker: ES
words: could have more freedom there, you know. And his sister had moved away

speaker: ES
words: by that time. She went, I think it was to Seattle, went to some people

speaker: ES
words: there and worked.

speaker: SS
words: Worked as a maid in their home? Worked in their home?

speaker: ES
words: Yes. So she never came back except she came once to visit them a while.

speaker: ES
words: So it was just that boy then.

speaker: SS
words: What do you think he thought of the way he was treated?

speaker: ES
words: The what?

speaker: SS
words: What do you think he thought of the way he was treated?

speaker: ES
words: Well, I don't know. He didn't take it very serious. He took it almost

speaker: ES
words: like if he was a slave. He was afraid to say anything, you know until

speaker: ES
words: he grew up. When he grew up he told his father off and then he left home.

speaker: ES
words: He got to working with a threshing outfit and working in the cookhouse

speaker: ES
words: and he took that up as a business. He had a restaurant in Elk River town

speaker: ES
words: after that. He cooked at some of the camps, you know they were logging

speaker: ES
words: ground Deary and up in there. And he cooked at the camps and then he

speaker: ES
words: settled in Elk River and he was at the head of that. He was doing Okay.

speaker: ES
words: And he was a pretty decent man. I saw him different times after he was

speaker: ES
words: fullgrown.

speaker: SS
words: Did he stop having anything to do with his fanner?

speaker: ES
words: Oh, yeah. Once I was in Elk River and that boy's dad came there to the

speaker: ES
words: restaurant for supper and the boy was civil to him, he just treated him

speaker: ES
words: like anybody else, but that was all. Well, his father died no so long

speaker: ES
words: after that. It was an accident. First his father was hauling stuff to

speaker: ES
words: Elk River and they had a railroad running in there, you know, and one

speaker: ES
words: part through the woods there, the railroad came up fairly close to the

speaker: ES
words: road; his team ran away and threw him out and broke his neck. That's a

speaker: ES
words: fact, it broke his neck. And they took him- they didn't have doctors in

speaker: ES
words: Elk River that could do anything, so they had a logging train in there

speaker: ES
words: and they took him to Bovill and then he was sent to Spokane. And it was

speaker: ES
words: such an unusual thing, I heard about it, that special doctors came^to

speaker: ES
words: Spokane to see this condition. They had to turn his head around. He told

speaker: ES
words: me afterwards, he said he could look down his back. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: So he recovered from that?

speaker: ES
words: And he recovered. I saw him after we came from Canada, he was still

speaker: ES
words: there at Park.

speaker: SS
words: But then what killed him?

speaker: ES
words: Well, later on, he fell into a scalding vat.

speaker: SS
words: For Hogs?

speaker: ES
words: Uh-huh. Yeah. And that finished— he lived a few days even then. But

speaker: ES
words: he was scalded so bad, I heard them say, that the flesh loosened from

speaker: ES
words: the bones in part of his body. But that killed him.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know how he could have fallen in?

speaker: ES
words: Well, I wasn't there, but you know a scalding vat is up off of the ground

speaker: ES
words: just a little bit, and we used to have that, too, you have a little bit

speaker: ES
words: of a platform then the full length of that and you put ropes around the

speaker: ES
words: hog and roll him into the scalding vat and then you pull him back with

speaker: ES
words: the ropes. And I don't know, somehow- whether he got tangled in the ropes

speaker: ES
words: or what, he fell into the trough there. That was up on Round Meadow

speaker: ES
words: if you know where that is. And they had to take him to Elk River before

speaker: ES
words: they had the doctor for him. They had to take him in there and he was

speaker: ES
words: shipped then to Spokane.

speaker: SS
words: Would a bunch of farmers get together at once to scald?

speaker: ES
words: Well, there wasn't many in there in Round Meadow, you know, but he had

speaker: ES
words: some help there. I helped him there one spring with the spring work af

speaker: ES
words: ter we came back from Canada. And they had hogs there then but we didn't

speaker: ES
words: do any butchering.

speaker: SS
words: This man- would he have been considered fairly well-to-do at that time?

speaker: ES
words: Well, speaking of how the times were then, - He sold his place at Park

speaker: ES
words: and he had this Round Meadow. He was in partnership with some man that

speaker: ES
words: had some money and he was running this. Whether he finally bought it or

speaker: ES
words: not, I don't know. But he sold his place at Park right after we came

speaker: ES
words: back from Canada and he got $5,000 for that. And at that time that was

speaker: ES
words: lots of money, you know. So they were kind of semiretired over there at

speaker: ES
words: Round Meadow. Well then, we moved away before he had that accident, so

speaker: ES
words: I don't know just what crowd he had, or what conditions were, but

speaker: ES
words: that's where he had that accident.

speaker: SS
words: I'm thinking more like; did they have enough money to have girls working

speaker: SS
words: for them? Staying at their house and working for them?

speaker: ES
words: Well, his wife at that time was quite capable to do their own housework.

speaker: ES
words: And two of their daughters were here in Moscow at that time. And after

speaker: ES
words: he died then she moved and stayed with her daughters here in Moscow. And

speaker: ES
words: she was a strong pioneer woman, you know and she could do the— I've stay

speaker: ES
words: ed with them when I helped them there; that was in 1926, I think it was,

speaker: ES
words: we moved back in '25, and it was right after that. Be about 1926. And

speaker: ES
words: she was a strong, healthy looking woman then. Fairly big woman. And

speaker: ES
words: she was stronger in proportion than he was.

speaker: SS
words: In most families, do you think that the parents were fairly sttict with

speaker: SS
words: their kids? Or how were they strict?

speaker: ES
words: Well, I can tell you about my own parents. My mother was a very good,

speaker: ES
words: mild mannered woman. Father was; he was kind of strict, but he never

speaker: ES
words: abused us, you know. Never did. But his word was law. But, of course,

speaker: ES
words: the way times were and the way we were kind of all by ourselves in Park

speaker: ES
words: there, we didn't get out much to town or to mix with other young folks

speaker: ES
words: very much, so we were kind of satisfied. We were all one kind of people

speaker: ES
words: in there, you might say, and so Father didn't have to be so strict.

speaker: ES
words: We didn't away at night and things like that. And we had cur little

speaker: ES
words: neighborhood dances in there and Christmas parties and that, and we were

speaker: ES
words: satisfied. But when I got old enough to begin to think for myself about

speaker: ES
words: how to do things and then he eased up quite a bit because he didn't un

speaker: ES
words: derstand what I was doing. I started to do a little carpenter work and

speaker: ES
words: things, and he didn't know the first thing about that so he didn't try

speaker: ES
words: to say what I should do. But up until that, why, he would tell me what

speaker: ES
words: to do, and of course, I did it. But he wasn't mean, you know, it wasn't

speaker: ES
words: that, he would scold sometimes.

speaker: SS
words: Did he expect you to do it the way he told you to? To do it his way?

speaker: ES
words: Not exactly, because about the only thing he understood was axe work;

speaker: ES
words: timber work, you know. Which was mostly what we were doing in those

speaker: ES
words: times. Fence posts and rails and stuff. We split rails and sold to

speaker: ES
words: some of the neighbors there, and that was a lot of work. Then we cleared

speaker: ES
words: land and things like that. And of course then, I worked for him, you

speaker: ES
words: know.

speaker: SS
words: What would cause him to get peeved at you? What would set him off?

speaker: ES
words: Well, if we shirked, you know, or anything like that, why he'd tell us to

speaker: ES
words: get busy. Or if we didn't do the chores up, he was right to the minute

speaker: ES
words: on chores, you know. And if we were a little slow on that he'd tell us to

speaker: ES
words: get busy. And we didn't argue with him. But as we grew older, why he be

speaker: ES
words: gan to kind of give us more freedom. So by the time I was fifteen, six

speaker: ES
words: teen years old, I knew about as much about the work there as he did, so

speaker: ES
words: we worked together more, and gradually, why, he'd kind of turn it ever to

speaker: ES
words: us. And of course, my brother older than I, he was four years older than

speaker: ES
words: I, so by the time I was half grown he was full grown and out on his own.

speaker: ES
words: So then it was just my father and I, and as time went along we got to be

speaker: ES
words: pretty good friends. He never abused us, really. And in a way we were

speaker: ES
words: better off than some of our neighbors because my parents were good, hon

speaker: ES
words: est, respectable people. And that was one thing that both he and Mother

speaker: ES
words: told us about; that we should be like that. And they told us how we

speaker: ES
words: would succeed if we lived that way.

speaker: SS
words: So they really tried to teach you to be honest and respectable.

speaker: ES
words: They were right up to snuff on that. And they showed us that's the way

speaker: ES
words: they lived. And when I got old enough to go with Father to town, when

speaker: ES
words: they'd go to Troy, that was before Deary was built, you know, he'd tell

speaker: ES
words: me a lot about things that he had to go through and told me how he made

speaker: ES
words: out, you know. And that stuck to me.

speaker: SS
words: Did he feel that the towns or places like Troy were probably places that

speaker: SS
words: people should steer clear of and try to stay away from?

speaker: ES
words: Well, the only thing was that he didn't like the saloons, you know, al

speaker: ES
words: though he took a drink once in a while himself. But he warned us about

speaker: ES
words: that; not to get mixed up in that. And they were religious, both Father

speaker: ES
words: and Mother and they taught that to us. And so when I'd go to town with

speaker: ES
words: him why, I would do the driving, you know, driving the horses, and when

speaker: ES
words: we get to town, why, he'd go and have a glass of beer or something right

speaker: ES
words: away, but I didn't go there. And we'd do our trading. We had plenty of

speaker: ES
words: time. We would get into town about ten o'clock. We'd leave home about

speaker: ES
words: three in the morning, and we'd let the horses rest then til about three

speaker: ES
words: or so in the afternoon to make the trip back home. And so, once in a

speaker: ES
words: while he'd maybe have one too many, but he was never cranky with me at

speaker: ES
words: that. So we had no trouble there.

speaker: SS
words: Did he tell you that life had been hard when he was growing up? Was

speaker: SS
words: that part of the way he was telling you how to—?

speaker: ES
words: No, no, that was just forgotten. And he began to ask me about this and

speaker: ES
words: that; how we should do it. And he wasn't so strict that I felt angry

speaker: ES
words: with him over the years. No. I kind of figured that it was about even

speaker: ES
words: up. Everything okay. Then some of the neighbors there they were a lit

speaker: ES
words: tle too lenient with their kids, and I could see that. Somehow or other

speaker: ES
words: I didn't— from the time I was quite young I didn't think it was right

speaker: ES
words: for kids to sass their parents, you know. And then have to suffer for

speaker: ES
words: it. So, my brother older than I he took it a little harder than I did

speaker: ES
words: because he was a little more independent, but then as soon as he grew up

speaker: ES
words: why- we didn't have trouble over it, not a least bit, we kind of thought

speaker: ES
words: Dad was hard with us at times, but as we grew older we could see that he

speaker: ES
words: had some reason for it. And of course, in those times the parents were

speaker: ES
words: kind of strict with their kids, most of them. Now the Helrud«£they had

speaker: ES
words: several children, and I noticed that- there were two brothers that had

speaker: ES
words: families there, you see- Helrude families, and one of them- the mother

speaker: ES
words: to the children was a little too easy with them, and she would brag

speaker: ES
words: them. The one Helrude had a boy two or three years younger than I and

speaker: ES
words: the other Helrude had a boy same age as I, and he was a bigger boy than

speaker: ES
words: I was, and his mother used to proud of telling about that once in a

speaker: ES
words: while, that he could do more than I could, you know. (Chuckles) And it

speaker: ES
words: kind of made him feel a little big until we were about half grown then

speaker: ES
words: we could figure things out together and we were good friends. We worked

speaker: ES
words: together and everything was okay. We never had any trouble.

speaker: SS
words: Well, these parents that were more lax with their kids; did they let

speaker: SS
words: their kids get off without working around the house?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah, they didn't do as much work. They would work some, but they had

speaker: ES
words: to kind of coax them more, you know. Now this boy would mind his father

speaker: ES
words: fairly good, his father didn't let him talk to him, but he was more

speaker: ES
words: easygoing on it. He didn't speak out quite so strict as my father did.

speaker: ES
words: You know my father went through military training in Norway, and that

speaker: ES
words: was kind of his life. And he was so precise about things. He even told

speaker: ES
words: me years later that he was given liberties in camp there, he had le arned

speaker: ES
words: to.butcher things and he got so that they let him kind of go out in

speaker: ES
words: the country and buy like, beefs, you know, bring them in and do the

speaker: ES
words: slaughtering and a kind of a roustabout when he didn't have to be in

speaker: ES
words: training. And he was so precise that when he finished there, he was

speaker: ES
words: given a silver watch. He used to say if he had an appointment to be

speaker: ES
words: at a certain place, he used to say, "It's better to be an hour early

speaker: ES
words: then to be a minute late." (Chuckles) And he got along good with his

speaker: ES
words: neighbors. If he was to help a neighbor you could depend he would be

speaker: ES
words: there.

speaker: SS
words: What about your mother? It sounds like she wasn't strict with you ex

speaker: SS
words: actly, and certainly not like your father was.

speaker: ES
words: She never slapped us or anything like that. When she'd tell us we did

speaker: ES
words: wrong we respected her :. She was good, and she was strong. We knew

speaker: ES
words: that she could handle us if she had to. (Chuckles) Oh, she was strong!

speaker: ES
words: Yeah, when they lived in Minnesota, when they had grain to haul to town

speaker: ES
words: sne'd go out and help, him, you know. Instead of the wheat sacks we had

speaker: ES
words: here they had cotton bags, they were narrower and taller, longer, and

speaker: ES
words: she could take one end and he'd take the other and throw it on the wagon.

speaker: ES
words: And I remember one day one of our cows kicked her; she took a stick and

speaker: ES
words: she beat up on that cow and she throwed the stick away and she sat down

speaker: ES
words: and milked that cow. (Chuckles) But otherwise, I never saw her really

speaker: ES
words: angry with us kids. She would be a little bit angry but, I mean, she never

speaker: ES
words: used hard language, no. She didn't need to because we minded her. And

speaker: ES
words: with the neighbors; there was one woman in the country that she didn't

speaker: ES
words: like so she didn't go and visit her, but she never sat around and talked

speaker: ES
words: bad about her; just left her alone.

speaker: SS
words: Was that a part of the life there for her to go visiting the neighbors?

speaker: ES
words: No. She didn't go around very much. She was always busy, but people

speaker: ES
words: were always welcome to come there. The neighbors would come there, why,

speaker: ES
words: she always was friends with them; even this woman that was so bad with

speaker: ES
words: her boy, she would come up to see Mother quite often. They were our near

speaker: ES
words: est neighbors; about a quarter of a mile apart. And many a time that

speaker: ES
words: woman- especially when her husband was away- she'd come up and spend quite

speaker: ES
words: a while with Mother. Yeah. They were good friends. So Mother didn't

speaker: ES
words: get angry with people, if they didn't do right, that wasn't her business.

speaker: ES
words: When they came there they were friends to her. She welcomed any of them

speaker: ES
words: that would come there.

speaker: SS
words: You say she was really busy with work.

speaker: ES
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Was work for her something she did all the time?

speaker: ES
words: Well, we had a big garden and then we milked three or four cows at one

speaker: ES
words: time and she helped with that. She did most of the garden work; I'd be

speaker: ES
words: with Father, you know. And she helped with the milking and all that, and

speaker: ES
words: did her own sewing and things like that, you know. So she was busy. And

speaker: ES
words: she was healthy. She was never to a doctor that I can remember and Fa

speaker: ES
words: ther the same way. You couldn't wish for better people for living way

speaker: ES
words: back in like that.

speaker: SS
words: I understand that for some women that was a very hard life and they didn't

speaker: SS
words: have very good health.

speaker: ES
words: Well, yes, there were a few, but most of 'em in there were pretty heal

speaker: ES
words: thy people. This one of the Helrudes he lost his wife. She got sick,

speaker: ES
words: and that was when I was quite young, so I don't even know what she was

speaker: ES
words: sick from. And it was a long ways to go and get a doctor, you know.

speaker: ES
words: And this family where he married the second time, they lost two babies,

speaker: ES
words: that I remember of shortly after they were born and they were buried out

speaker: ES
words: there a little ways from the house.

speaker: SS
words: Would you go to your mother if you wanted something like shoes or some

speaker: SS
words: thing special? Would she be the one you would ask for-

speaker: ES
words: Clothing?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: ES
words: Oh, no. We would ask Father about that and we didn't have to ask, he fur

speaker: ES
words: nished us and we were satisfied. He bought boots for us for in the win

speaker: ES
words: ter sometimes and when spring came they weren't wore out so we cut the

speaker: ES
words: tops off and used them as shoes, you know. No, we were never suffering

speaker: ES
words: for that.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard in a lot of families- and I don*t know if this would be true

speaker: SS
words: of yours and those you knew- but I heard a lot of families, the father—

speaker: SS
words: the mother would be affectionate with the kids, you know, she would show

speaker: SS
words: affection and the father really didn't, he just didn't feel that was

speaker: SS
words: right, and that the mother did. Is that pretty much the way it was with

speaker: SS
words: you?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. Well, one of those families, it was a little that way; this one

speaker: ES
words: that bragged of her boy, you know, was a little that way, but not very

speaker: ES
words: bad. So far as I know they worked together pretty good. And it almost

speaker: ES
words: had to be with pioneers like that, you know, they had to depend on

speaker: ES
words: each other pretty much and work together.

speaker: SS
words: Did they have dances in Park before you left?

speaker: ES
words: Oh, we had a few, yeah, we had a few.

speaker: SS
words: I was going to ask you what your parents taught you about religion.

speaker: ES
words: Well, they were strict Lutherans, you know, and so were the neighbors

speaker: ES
words: there. Nearly all of them. There was one neighbor that was Methodist

speaker: ES
words: and he was a little better, you might say educated about religion, so

speaker: ES
words: they chose him for Sunday School teacher. The others were Lutheran;

speaker: ES
words: he was Methodist, but he taught the Sunday School there.

speaker: SS
words: Was he Norwegian?

speaker: ES
words: Oh, yes. They weren't really radical about it, you know. It was more a

speaker: ES
words: case of being a good neighborhood.

speaker: SS
words: Did they think— did your parents teach you that religion was supposed

speaker: SS
words: to be important in your life?

speaker: ES
words: Oh, yes. And of course, as we grew up we had no occasion to consider

speaker: ES
words: or think about any other religion than the Lutheran. We didn't get out

speaker: ES
words: to mix with others very much. I can remember when we were confirmed;

speaker: ES
words: the minister gave us a little talk and advice, you know. And he said,

speaker: ES
words: "Soon you will be out on your own, among other people, and you will mix

speaker: ES
words: with people and hear their side of things and in religion," he said,"they

speaker: ES
words: will try to win you over. But don't listen to them." I thought to my

speaker: ES
words: self, "Don't worry, I'm not going to listen to them." (Chuckles) But

speaker: ES
words: as I got out and began to get acquainted with others I found they were

speaker: ES
words: good people too. We never had any trouble with anybody over religion,

speaker: ES
words: not at all, because we began to understand that there was two sides to

speaker: ES
words: it with most of them.

speaker: SS
words: Can you tell me just a little bit of what you learned when you grew up

speaker: SS
words: and what the Lutheran teaching was?

speaker: ES
words: In what way?

speaker: SS
words: In how to live your life, and God was—

speaker: ES
words: We got that through our Sunday School and then the minister he would

speaker: ES
words: come in there once a month and we had lessons to go through with him,

speaker: ES
words: you know. We would have in our books, we would have several pages for

speaker: ES
words: that one time. So we practically had learned that so we could answer

speaker: ES
words: any question that he would ask us. And that was what we believed in

speaker: ES
words: and that was it. In fact we didn't have any chance to do much wrong because

speaker: ES
words: we were all under this strict religion. And at that time everybody

speaker: ES
words: believed his neighbor was honest and we never had any trouble about any

speaker: ES
words: tools or anything like that being missed, you know. Our barns and houses

speaker: ES
words: and woodsheds and so on was open to anybody to go in that wanted to, and

speaker: ES
words: nothing was ever missed.

speaker: SS
words: Did people talke about religion among themselves and discuss it?

speaker: ES
words: Oh, to some extent they did, but they didn't make much of an issue about

speaker: ES
words: it, they all believed the same way and that was it. There was one Adventist

speaker: ES
words: family that lived in there and he neighbored with us and there was

speaker: ES
words: no trouble. And at one time an Adventist minister came in there to visit

speaker: ES
words: them, you know and he had asked our school directors if he could have

speaker: ES
words: a meeting in the schoolhouse, and they gave him permission. So he came

speaker: ES
words: in to our Sunday School one Sunday; he came in to announce this. He

speaker: ES
words: came in and he sat down back by the door and was quiet til Sunday School

speaker: ES
words: was over and then he got up and asked permission to speak and then he

speaker: ES
words: announced that he had been given permission to have services there the

speaker: ES
words: next Saturday, and he invited us to come. And I was looking at the peo

speaker: ES
words: ple and at him when he said this and I could see some of them was giving

speaker: ES
words: him pretty hard looks there; they figured he was a bad man, you know.

speaker: ES
words: (Chuckles) And of course, they didn't go to his services.

speaker: SS
words: Did anyone go besides that Adventist family?

speaker: ES
words: I don't know. We didn't go, and I don't know about the others. I don't

speaker: ES
words: think many went there, because when people are drilled into believing one

speaker: ES
words: certain thing for a long time, they don't want to believe anything else.

speaker: SS
words: I was wondering just a little bit of what the Lutheran teaching was, what

speaker: SS
words: it taught you about religion.

speaker: ES
words: Well, I don't see any big difference; the Adventists believe, of course,

speaker: ES
words: in Saturday being the holiday. And there were certain foods that they

speaker: ES
words: didn't think they should eat; and that was about it. And in what I have

speaker: ES
words: read and learned since those times, I think it was almost silly that they

speaker: ES
words: should argue about things which were not so important. Now, Saturday

speaker: ES
words: being a holiday, that was almost more correct than having Sunday. Be

speaker: ES
words: cause if you go into the Bible about it, Christ kept the seventh day, you

speaker: ES
words: know and his disciples did. And I haven't found any place in the Bible

speaker: ES
words: where it says that they kept Sunday, I mean, His disciples. They met

speaker: ES
words: on Sunday in remembrance of His rising back to life, you know. They kept

speaker: ES
words: that a special day, but it don't say that they didn't keep the Sabbath.

speaker: ES
words: So there you are.

speaker: SS
words: What about dancing? I've understood that some churches were against

speaker: SS
words: dancing; they didn't want them to dance.

speaker: ES
words: Oh, yeah. My parents were and most of the neighbors there were. They

speaker: ES
words: had been brought up to believe that having dances and being jolly and

speaker: ES
words: all that, and to a great extent they thought it was not right. And

speaker: ES
words: they didn't have a chance to look at both sides enough to— I remember

speaker: ES
words: when I was around seventeen, eighteen years old, I got an accordion, and

speaker: ES
words: I learned to play that some. That was okay, but later I got a violin,

speaker: ES
words: and my mother didn't think that was good, because they used violins at

speaker: ES
words: dances. So, I had my room upstairs and that's where I*d play my violin.

speaker: ES
words: And I got so I could play some of the hymns we used in Sunday School.

speaker: ES
words: So I took the violin down and I sat down and I played one of them to Mo

speaker: ES
words: ther. That settled it; the violin was okay! (Chuckles) So, you see

speaker: ES
words: they would have one line of thought and that carried through from their

speaker: ES
words: parents to them and on, you know.

speaker: SS
words: But then when it reached your generation it changed a little bit, because

speaker: SS
words: you - didn't you start seeing these other ways? You must have had some

speaker: SS
words: reason to get a violin.

speaker: ES
words: Well, I like music, and I didn't consider any music instrument being dif

speaker: ES
words: ferent than another. So, you get a little more open-minded as you grow

speaker: ES
words: up. And it didn't matter to me, as long as it was music. And dances,

speaker: ES
words: now, mv parents didn't really - she didn't condemn then, but still she

speaker: ES
words: thought it wasn't really religious, you know. But when we grew up and

speaker: ES
words: we began to go dancing, why, they didn't object to it.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have dances at Park, or did you go mostly other places to dance?

speaker: ES
words: We had dances around home. You know in the wintertime sometimes roads

speaker: ES
words: would be pretty bad and we didn't get outside. We supplied up with gro

speaker: ES
words: ceries for the winter and we were back there through the winter. But,

speaker: ES
words: now, like this man I told about married the second time, he had the

speaker: ES
words: biggest house in the district so we had dances there. And we were friends,

speaker: ES
words: young folks were. We didn't regard anything like the old people did

speaker: ES
words: about things, so we were good friends and we had dances together.

speaker: SS
words: Where do you think the young folks got the idea that it was alright to

speaker: SS
words: dance, if the parents -

speaker: ES
words: We just naturally couldn't see anything wrong with it. You know. Now

speaker: ES
words: if anybody got drunk at the dance, we didn't stand for that. Oh, no.

speaker: ES
words: That would be wrong. Or if the fellow got to flirting too much with some

speaker: ES
words: of the girls, why, we didn't think that was right. So they didn't get

speaker: ES
words: away with something like that. So it was quite peaceful. Okay.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have get-to-gathers at homes that were just parties and not dan

speaker: SS
words: ces?

speaker: ES
words: Oh, yes. Yeah. Sometimes after Sunday School a neighbor would have the

speaker: ES
words: rest of them invited to come to their place, you know. And maybe another

speaker: ES
words: time to some other place. We had good visits together. 'Course, we boys,

speaker: ES
words: if that was in the summer we weren't in the house much, you know, we'd

speaker: ES
words: be out either chasing squirrels or something. They put up good dinners

speaker: ES
words: you know.

speaker: SS
words: Would you play party games?

speaker: ES
words: Well, we started playing cards a little bit, and then there were other

speaker: ES
words: games that parents had taught the children, you know. Indoor games. I

speaker: ES
words: don't remember much of it now. There wasn't so much of that. There was

speaker: ES
words: some games where— see, what was it? I remember one game that you had to

speaker: ES
words: forfeit if you missed the answer, you know, you had to forfeit something.

speaker: ES
words: I got stuck in one of them. I was kind of going with a girl, but it

speaker: ES
words: wasn't very serious, but at the same time she looked better to me than

speaker: ES
words: the rest, and at this party, why, I didn't get the right answer and so

speaker: ES
words: the penalty was- they were lined up there kind of- you should startthe

speaker: ES
words: girls were all by themselves here and the boys - so then you were

speaker: ES
words: to go to these and you should bow to the wittiest and kneel to the pret

speaker: ES
words: tiest and kiss the one you loved best. (Chuckles) Boy, was I in a cor

speaker: ES
words: ner! And I was kind of bashful, too, in those times. (Laughter) Some

speaker: ES
words: thing turned up and my girl was there, and oh, I would rather take a

speaker: ES
words: licking than go over and kiss her, you know! (Laughter) And something

speaker: ES
words: turned up before I got started on that, I don't know what it was, but it

speaker: ES
words: broke up the game. That saved my life that time. (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: Would you have kissed her if you had to?

speaker: ES
words: I s'pose I would have had to. I thought at first that I'd kneel to her

speaker: ES
words: as the prettiest and kiss one of the other girls, but I didn't get to

speaker: ES
words: it!

speaker: SS
words: Boy! That's a mean one, isn't it?

speaker: ES
words: Boy,that was a fgyqhonel They.teased the life out of me afterwardsl

speaker: SS
words: It sounds like games like that were a chance to get to pair off and get

speaker: SS
words: to know the girl.

speaker: ES
words: Yes, yes. And one game- they'd pair you off sometimes— one game was

speaker: ES
words: that you were to go into another room and you were not told what was

speaker: ES
words: going to happen. You were going to go in another room and they fixed

speaker: ES
words: up two chairs and they strung a blanket over there real tight and two

speaker: ES
words: girls sat down on those chairs and there was a space between those chairs

speaker: ES
words: and underneath that blanket was a tub full of water, or part full of wa

speaker: ES
words: ter, and it was backed like this and the blanket would go down to the

speaker: ES
words: floor across from those chairs so when you came in you didn't know about

speaker: ES
words: this. And then you were supposed to come in and you were supposed to

speaker: ES
words: talk to those ladies, say, "Hello," and so on, and then they would ask

speaker: ES
words: you to sit down, you seeibetween them! That'd put you right in the tub!

speaker: ES
words: Because when you sat down, why, they'd get up, you know and the blan

speaker: ES
words: ket would go into the tub! I didn't get caught in that. (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that the girls were raised any different than the boys, as

speaker: SS
words: far as the boys having more freedom? They could go around and do more

speaker: SS
words: than the girls?

speaker: ES
words: Yes, of course. The boys- we could go from one place to another, but

speaker: ES
words: not very many of the girls would go alone. They would come- like when

speaker: ES
words: my younger sister was home, why, there'd be other girls come to visit her.

speaker: ES
words: And sometimes they'd go to another place together, but not as much as the

speaker: ES
words: boys would. But there was very few of us boys old enough to really pick

speaker: ES
words: out a special girl to go with. And if did, why, he'd better stay

speaker: ES
words: home pretty well, because the whole neighborhood would find out, you know

speaker: ES
words: and they'd just tease you to death! (Chuckles) So, there wasn't much of

speaker: ES
words: that going on.

speaker: SS
words: If you wanted to go out with a girl, where could you take her anyway?

speaker: ES
words: Well, that s it, there was nowhere to go, unless you want to go to some

speaker: ES
words: other neighbor with her. So there wasn't much of that done. And we

speaker: ES
words: didn't have any buggies; we had just wagons, you know, until the very

speaker: ES
words: last part, so, you couldn't take a girl out to town or anything like that.

speaker: ES
words: One Christmas a bunch of us young folks got together; I furnished a team

speaker: ES
words: of horses and the neighbor furnished a bobsled and we all got into that

speaker: ES
words: and went clear down to what was called Nora, down there to a Christmas

speaker: ES
words: doing. We left right after noon; got down there in time for the doings

speaker: ES
words: and then back home at night. And we got caught in this snowstorm going

speaker: ES
words: back home. So, before we started out, why,- there was three of us boys

speaker: ES
words: and four girls, and we arranged that that one should drive all the way

speaker: ES
words: going down and going back at night the other two should share the driving,

speaker: ES
words: and I drove all the way going down, so the other two had to set up there

speaker: ES
words: in the snowstorm and drive the horses. And my girl was in that party,

speaker: ES
words: and we had a lot of blankets with us, and we pulled a blanket over us

speaker: ES
words: so we were in the dry! And of course, the other girls they'd set there

speaker: ES
words: and kick at us and tease us, you know! (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: It sounds to me kind of like that most things were done in groups.

speaker: ES
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: A group of boys and a group of girls.

speaker: ES
words: That's right.

speaker: SS
words: I suppose that if a boy and a girl went off by themselves then it was

speaker: SS
words: pretty serious. For instance, if they went into town, just the two of

speaker: SS
words: them together or something like that, that would be more serious cour

speaker: SS
words: ting.

speaker: ES
words: Yah, it could have been. If we'd had a way of going, I suppose we would

speaker: ES
words: have. But the girls didn't do much horseback riding, for one thing, the

speaker: ES
words: boys had horses we'd ride, you know. The girls wouldn't. So they didn't

speaker: ES
words: get out of the neighborhood except to go to town for shopping. So we

speaker: ES
words: spent the winters pretty much right back in there.

speaker: SS
words: Were the girls expected to work as hard as the boys?

speaker: EA
words: Oh, no. No. They helped with the housework. They never did much work

speaker: EA
words: outside, except in the garden and tend to the chickens and things like

speaker: EA
words: that.

speaker: SS
words: But they worked?

speaker: ES
words: Oh, yah. And they were all strong and healthy so they could do heavy work

speaker: ES
words: but we never had them out with farm work. Well, this outlaw I speak about^

speaker: ES
words: he had the older girls out helping to shock the grain and such and help

speaker: ES
words: in the hay, some, but they were big strong girls, they could do it.

speaker: SS
words: But that was kind of unusual?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah, they were about the only ones that had to go out and when they got

speaker: ES
words: older, why, then the older ones would get out to work, you know. And

speaker: ES
words: then the younger boys grew up, so they didn't have to do much of that.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that your generation, you know boys that were growing up

speaker: SS
words: when you were, do you think that they believed that there was a really

speaker: SS
words: good chance to get ahead- you know they talk about making a lot of moneyin

speaker: SS
words: those days did people think they could make a lot of money?

speaker: ES
words: No. We didn't think much of that. We thought more about getting a good

speaker: ES
words: job of some kind that would- that we would earn money at. And speaking

speaker: ES
words: for myself, why, I never thought about getting rich or anything like that-

speaker: ES
words: I never did. I was more anxious to get a- what I first liked to have had

speaker: ES
words: was an education. But as conditions were, I was there with my parents,

speaker: ES
words: and of course, I had to stay with them, you know. So the next thing was

speaker: ES
words: to learn a trade, and I liked carpenter work. So I began to kind of work

speaker: ES
words: towards that, together with the farming. And I liked farm work, too, but

speaker: ES
words: I kind of liked to do more particular work like carpenter work.

speaker: SS
words: How did you get a chance to learn carpentry?

speaker: ES
words: My older brother farmed about thirty, thirty-five miles from us and I

speaker: ES
words: was over there once and he had a horse that I liked better than mine,

speaker: ES
words: and I talked him into making a trade.

speaker: SS
words: You gave him the horse, and you gave him—

speaker: ES
words: I was to give him twenty dollars to boot on horses.

speaker: SS
words: And you didn't have that?

speaker: ES
words: And I didn't have the money, but he needed my help. He had a carpenter

speaker: ES
words: there; they were just starting to build a barn on his place, a big barn.

speaker: ES
words: And he needed someone to help this carpenter, and I liked that kind of

speaker: ES
words: work. I had done just a little of it before, so I got to work with him

speaker: ES
words: those twenty days. I got a dollar a day, you know. And he was a very

speaker: ES
words: good man to work with, he would take time to show me how to do things;

speaker: ES
words: to do it right. So by the time we got through with that I could do

speaker: ES
words: some carpentry work. And then back home we had a little bit to finish

speaker: ES
words: on the house and I did that and then we built the barn, and I did that.

speaker: ES
words: And then I did a little work for some of the neighbors. I worked on the

speaker: ES
words: church that we built; volunteer work some, and things like that. And

speaker: ES
words: then eventually I moved over to where my brother lived and farmed there.

speaker: ES
words: I did some work there, I built the belfry on the schoolhouse there and

speaker: ES
words: things like that.

speaker: SS
words: Did you learn most of it on your own, or did you learn most of it from

speaker: SS
words: other people? Watching.

speaker: ES
words: No. I worked on my own. After I got started it seemed to kind of come

speaker: ES
words: natural for me to do this kind of work, and of course, I studied it as

speaker: ES
words: I went along. Didn't try to do more than I understood to do . And from

speaker: ES
words: then on through life, why- well, I built the kitchen onto the house we

speaker: ES
words: had on the farm we bought, and so I made a little money there selling

speaker: ES
words: that. Added onto the barn and things. And I built an addition to my

speaker: ES
words: brother's house. And so, everytime I worked on a job I learned a little,

speaker: ES
words: you know, through trial and error.

speaker: SS
words: I was going to ask you how far the school did go at Park. Did it go all

speaker: SS
words: the way up to the eighth grade?

speaker: ES
words: No. No, the children didn't stay home long enough for that. Of course,

speaker: ES
words: they may have done that after we left, you know. They built the new

speaker: ES
words: schoolhouse and they may have gone further. I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: How far did it go? For most kids?

speaker: ES
words: Well, I got through the fourth grade and by that time my older brother

speaker: ES
words: was leaving home and got married and so I had to help Father. At that

speaker: ES
words: time you weren't supposed to go a certain grade, you know, when you

speaker: ES
words: got old enough to work and you could add and subtract, you were through.

speaker: SS
words: How old were you then?

speaker: ES
words: I was about fourteen years old. I was nine years old when I started

speaker: ES
words: school, and I was about fourteen- I went through about one grade a

speaker: ES
words: year.

speaker: ES
words: When you were in school, did you have to lay out much to help? To Work?

speaker: ES
words: While you were going to school?

speaker: ES
words: Oh, yeah, we had morning and evenings and weekends. I worked at home,

speaker: ES
words: I never worked away from home in those days except when harvest was on,

speaker: ES
words: why then, the neighbors got together and helped each other.

speaker: ES
words: Did you ever have have to stay out of school during the week?

speaker: ES
words: No. Not— you see we had our school through the winter months. And at

speaker: ES
words: first we had only four months of school; winter and early spring, then

speaker: ES
words: school was out.

speaker: SS
words: So it didn't interfere with the heavy work?

speaker: ES
words: No. We had a small farm. Father managed most of that and I got through

speaker: ES
words: in time for spring work, you know. And harvest.

speaker: SS
words: Had there been any school before- where you lived before you were nine?

speaker: SS
words: Before you came out here, had there been a school?

speaker: ES
words: No. I went to school a few days in Minnesota; I think it was a week.

speaker: ES
words: We left there in the spring, you know, and I went with my older brother

speaker: ES
words: to school. I can remember that. It wasn't long enough that I learned

speaker: ES
words: much, so I was practically a new beginner when we started in Idaho.

speaker: SS
words: Well, did they have school when you first came here? It was a little

speaker: SS
words: later they started.

speaker: ES
words: that's why. I was seven and a half years old when we came and Father

speaker: ES
words: got a house built that summer; cabin, and we moved into that. There was

speaker: ES
words: no schoolhouse. Then they had to build a schoolhouse next, and that took

speaker: ES
words: the next summer to build that. That's why I was about nine when I started school.

speaker: SS
words: I was going to ask you about— When you left Park and you went to Cen

speaker: SS
words: tral Ridge; is that because there wasn't enough land around that you

speaker: SS
words: could get to farm? To make a living?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. We still didn't have outbroke up. There was timber there, you

speaker: ES
words: know. And I rented a farm. The last three years we were at Park, I

speaker: ES
words: rented a farm from a bachelor neighbor and I began to make a little money

speaker: ES
words: there, but I still couldn t see a future ahead there, and I had a lot of

speaker: ES
words: ambition. And a neighbor of my brother over on the Ridge there, he of

speaker: ES
words: fered his place for sale, and so I talked to my brother about this pajlce

speaker: ES
words: at Park. I got an idea he could afford to buy it maybe, and so I put a

speaker: ES
words: price on it of $2,500 and we talked about it and he got kind of interested

speaker: ES
words: and I said,we should keep that in the family. There was my father's home

speaker: ES
words: stead where he spent a lot of his and Mother's life, and they would hate

speaker: ES
words: to see a stranger come in there on that place. So I suggested to him, I

speaker: ES
words: said, "If you'll buy it, I sell it to you on good terms, and that'll

speaker: ES
words: help me to pay for what I^buy." So he decided he would. So he bought

speaker: ES
words: that place, and he paid down some and^to borrow money to make a downpayment

speaker: ES
words: on the place that I bought. I went in debt, but it was good land

speaker: ES
words: there. So we made out Okay. And that's why that place at Park is still

speaker: ES
words: you might say, in the family. One of my nephews, my oldest brother's son

speaker: ES
words: has it now. So, it stayed in the family.

speaker: SS
words: That's Stiner?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. That's him.

speaker: SS
words: Now this brother of yours, the oldest brother- what was his name?

speaker: ES
words: Sven.

speaker: SS
words: And that's Helmer's father too?

speaker: ES
words: Ya. That's right.

speaker: SS
words: So you went over to Central Ridge then and started to farm there?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. We farmed there about, I think it was about five years. Yeah.

speaker: ES
words: I rented out for two years. I got the idea I wanted to go to Canada and

speaker: ES
words: raise cattle, you know. There was a lot of that done up there before

speaker: ES
words: my time and I thought there was still a place to get there so when I had

speaker: ES
words: a chance to sell I sold mine. My folks were living with my oldest brother

speaker: ES
words: then there, you know, so that was okay.

speaker: SS
words: But at Central Ridge why did you wind up deciding that that wasn't worth

speaker: SS
words: the effort?

speaker: ES
words: Well, farming was okay and I was making money enough.to out my contract

speaker: ES
words: on there, but I had this idea that I could make money faster by having a

speaker: ES
words: cattle ranch. Up there, you know, you could lease land from the govern

speaker: ES
words: ment if you took a homestead but you had to raise hay to feed 'em about

speaker: ES
words: half of the year up in the north country. There wasn't any prairie coun

speaker: ES
words: try there. There could be big meadows and things and that's where you

speaker: ES
words: Peace River

speaker: ES
words: would get your hay. Well I scoured all that country and all that

speaker: ES
words: up in there and I couldn't find a place where there was enough hay for

speaker: ES
words: cattle. I even had a deal made for forty head of these Black Angus cat

speaker: ES
words: tle; heifers, ready to go right in there, but I couldn't buy 'em until I

speaker: ES
words: had a place for them.

speaker: SS
words: Where were you looking? What place was this you were looking?

speaker: ES
words: I went north from Edmonton up in there, you know, I went east after that

speaker: ES
words: but I went north up into what's called the Peace River country. And up

speaker: ES
words: there I met three cattlemen from- two from Montana and one from Southern

speaker: ES
words: Alberta that was looking for land. So the three of us, we went on a

speaker: ES
words: Boat on the Peace River clear way up into that north country there. And

speaker: ES
words: we finally found a place. There was a French Canadian man there that

speaker: ES
words: knew the country and he had a team and wagon and he took us out there.

speaker: ES
words: That took us a day and a half one way, out in the wilderness, and there

speaker: ES
words: was a big prairie there, open land, and we all could file and we all

speaker: ES
words: could get located there together, and not one of us decided to stay.

speaker: ES
words: For one thing, it was an awful road to get out there. In one plaffwe

speaker: ES
words: run into what they call a muskeg, and we had to unhook the horses and

speaker: ES
words: get to the land and run a cable back to pull the wagon through. And

speaker: ES
words: another thing- and of course, you had to build. And the winters were

speaker: ES
words: long and hard. And I got to thinking; I kind of was getting towards get

speaker: ES
words: ting married too, at that time, how would it be if you got out there and

speaker: ES
words: got sick? Even the town we went out from was just a couple of stores

speaker: ES
words: and that was about it. And the steamboats went up there only twice a

speaker: ES
words: year. But we had heard about this wonderland, you know, but we didn't

speaker: ES
words: know about these harships you might go through. So all of us went right

speaker: ES
words: back again.

speaker: SS
words: None of you filed?

speaker: ES
words: None of us even- we found a section corner- they had iron stakes for each

speaker: ES
words: section corner there- and we stood there and looked around and wasn't

speaker: ES
words: much said for quite a while, finally one of 'em turned to me and he said,

speaker: ES
words: "What do you say, Ed?" I said, "No, you can have it." The others said,

speaker: ES
words: "No, we don't want it." So we went back and then I began to look for

speaker: ES
words: farmland rangeland close by. Iwent east and on in towards Saskatch

speaker: ES
words: ewan, in there and different places. I found a place; I bought about a

speaker: ES
words: section of land there and there was pastureland by it there.

speaker: SS
words: Was it difficult to find any place that wasn't taken?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah, it was. That is, that looked good. And that was towards harvest

speaker: ES
words: time and the crops looked good there. The price was good on wheat and

speaker: ES
words: grain. And I bought one quarter section for $1,250 from a land company,

speaker: ES
words: that was all raw land, of course. And I bought another quarter section

speaker: ES
words: had some broken up on it, and had a crop on it, but he claimed the crop.

speaker: ES
words: I got that for $1,600. Then there was a homestead right by there that

speaker: ES
words: was pastureland and there was two springs on it and part of the Battle

speaker: ES
words: River run through one corner of it. I got that as a homestead by- you

speaker: ES
words: could homestead without living on it if you had land adjoining it. And

speaker: ES
words: you had to put a certain number of cattle on there, and that would prove

speaker: ES
words: it up for you. Which I did. And then there was another quarter section

speaker: ES
words: there that I didn't buy right away, and my brother came up there and he

speaker: ES
words: said, "Say," he says, "I'm going to buy that." He lived in southern Al

speaker: ES
words: berta. He bought that one. And he thought maybe his son would want to

speaker: ES
words: come up in there, you know. But he wouldn't go up there, so then later

speaker: ES
words: on I bought that. But the first two years we didn't get a thing. The

speaker: ES
words: first year I put in what I had on mine and I rented a place and I farmed

speaker: ES
words: that, and when the grain was about waist high it froze. Just froze so

speaker: ES
words: it just wilted down. We got a little bit of hay out of it, but it just

speaker: ES
words: fell flat on the ground. It froze ice on the water outside there, in a

speaker: ES
words: bucket I had, so you know how it was.

speaker: SS
words: What month of the year, would that be?

speaker: ES
words: It was in July. And I had got married in the meantime. And so the next

speaker: ES
words: year I went to work again at it and that time it froze again, but we

speaker: ES
words: could get hay out of it, and I sold one load of oats. That was all I

speaker: ES
words: got that year. A small load. And I had to buy seed grain again. And

speaker: ES
words: we began to have children, that was a little extra expense, you know,

speaker: ES
words: and I had to build onto the house. Well that year, that third year, I

speaker: ES
words: had one load of wheat to sell. And, I'll tell you that was hard times,

speaker: ES
words: now. So, we began to talk about Idaho. And I began to break up land

speaker: ES
words: as fast as I could afford it. So I had about a hundred acres more in

speaker: ES
words: crops the last year I was there. And I figured out a way to sell- no

speaker: ES
words: body had money to buy with to amount to anything, you know, and I had

speaker: ES
words: a neighbor there that wanted to buy; so I figured out if I would sell

speaker: ES
words: almost on the same way as you rent your place; sell on crop payments.

speaker: ES
words: Because if you take a mortgage on a piece of land and he don't have a

speaker: ES
words: crop, what's he going to pay with? So you're just as safe on crop pay

speaker: ES
words: ment as you are on a mortgage. And the contract was-ir he failed when

speaker: ES
words: he had the crop, if he failed to pay I could give him a certain length

speaker: ES
words: of time notice and I could repossess the land. So, we sold. I sold to

speaker: ES
words: two different people there, and we moved back to Idaho.

speaker: SS
words: How many years were you up there?

speaker: ES
words: Well, we were there from 1918 to 1925.

speaker: SS
words: When it was hard times, after you lost those couple of crops, how diffi

speaker: SS
words: cult was it for you?

speaker: ES
words: Well, you see, you couldn't raise anything else but grain there. We had

speaker: ES
words: good hay land; we could cut what they called the buffalo grass, we could

speaker: ES
words: cut that for hay. So I didn't have to buy hay for my horses and cattle.

speaker: SS
words: Well, could you grow good gardens and that sort of thing there, or not?

speaker: ES
words: No, we could grow potatoes and carrots and cabbage, but it would be apt

speaker: ES
words: to freeze, you know. One year my potatoes froze down twice. (Chuckles)

speaker: ES
words: I tell you, it took a strong back to face up to it! And I bought a few

speaker: ES
words: cattle to prove up that homestead. I paid up to a hundred dollars a

speaker: ES
words: head for cows; fifty dollars for calves. And by the time we were ready

speaker: ES
words: to sell out, I had to sell what cattle I had for two cents a pound! Just

speaker: ES
words: imagine that! Shipped them to Edmonton. A couple of us got together

speaker: ES
words: and shipped carload lots, you know, and we sent a man up there to sell

speaker: ES
words: them. During that time, just before I sold, I went to a sale and there

speaker: ES
words: was a nice, big, red cow there; I bought her for fifteen dollars. A

speaker: ES
words: man came a little late to the sale, he came to buy that cow, and he was

speaker: ES
words: disappointed, so he offered me twenty dollars and I sold the cow to

speaker: ES
words: him. (Chuckles) I paid two hundred dollars a head- no, I paid $750 for

speaker: ES
words: four horses and when I sold them I had twelve horses and I sold the bunch

speaker: ES
words: for $500; was all I got for 'em.

speaker: SS
words: What happened to the market? What was going on? Was the price on every

speaker: SS
words: thing just going down?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. You see, I came there the last year of the war, that is, that I

speaker: ES
words: was settled, in 1918, and the price was up. Wheat was two dollars a

speaker: ES
words: bushel. I had to buy grain; seed grain, three years before I had a crop.

speaker: ES
words: And oats, the same way; was high. Horses and cattle was high. And then

speaker: ES
words: all at once, it began to go down, and it just kept going down. I heard

speaker: ES
words: about one cattle rancher that got in so deep that he just walked off and

speaker: ES
words: left everything. Left his herd of cattle and everything. He couldn't

speaker: ES
words: commence to break even, so he just left. Oh, I'll tell you, there was

speaker: ES
words: many of 'em that lived on almost nothing there.

speaker: SS
words: Did it get bad enough that you worried about having enough to eat?

speaker: ES
words: It was down pretty close. We had a little blackboard en the wall in the

speaker: ES
words: kitchen and my wife would write down what she'd need, because I'd go to

speaker: ES
words: town maybe once a week, and before I'd go to town I'd sit down and write

speaker: ES
words: down on a piece of paper what I was gonna buy, you know, and we'd try to

speaker: ES
words: figure out what we could do without that was on the blackboard. And she

speaker: ES
words: was so good about saving things and not worrying about times. I worried

speaker: ES
words: more than she did. She was so good and she could make clothes for the

speaker: ES
words: children, you know. They all were born there in that house. And so we

speaker: ES
words: got along. If she hadn't have been as helpful as she was, it would have

speaker: ES
words: been rough.

speaker: SS
words: Was she used to that from her childhood? To roughing it?

speaker: ES
words: Well, pretty much. They lived very economically; but they lived good.

speaker: ES
words: She was the only child, and her mother died the year before we were mar

speaker: ES
words: ried. So her father came to live with us up there.

speaker: SS
words: She was a Brica ?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. That's right, you know. Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: So, she had a lot of courage.

speaker: ES
words: Oh, you bet she did.

speaker: SS
words: During hard times.

speaker: ES
words: She did, but she was willing. I had just a little two room cabin when

speaker: ES
words: we married. But the next spring we built on. Not only that, but we

speaker: ES
words: had to build a schoolhouse. There was no school there, you know. And

speaker: ES
words: some of my neighbors met at my place, that was the fall before I was mar

speaker: ES
words: ried, to organize. And they chose me as one of the members, and then

speaker: ES
words: we had to get a contract with someone to build a schoolhouse. And there

speaker: ES
words: wasn't very many that had much of a house, but several of my neighbors

speaker: ES
words: had more house than we had, and they wouldn't board the carpenters. They

speaker: ES
words: came, some of them, from— The main carpenter was from Edmonton, and he

speaker: ES
words: hired some. Well, I was on the board; it was up to me and the others,

speaker: ES
words: you know, to see that they got a place to stay. One neighbor there had

speaker: ES
words: a nice two-story house built about the time I moved in there, and they

speaker: ES
words: wouldn't take 'em. Well, we had just this little place there and they

speaker: ES
words: came down to me, said they had to get a place to stay or they couldn't

speaker: ES
words: built the school. So I asked my wife, I said— and we had just the little

speaker: ES
words: twelve-by-twelve sl^ed kitchen and the bedroom. But this neighbor that

speaker: ES
words: had the big place, they had a cookhouse and I figured if we could manage

speaker: ES
words: to board those fellows, I'd bring the cookhouse down and they could sleep

speaker: ES
words: there. So, I asked my wife, yeah, said she'd tackle it. There was a

speaker: ES
words: couple of neighbors that helped, so there wasn't so many of 'em stayed;

speaker: ES
words: four or five, something like that.

speaker: SS
words: How come these other neighbors didn't want to help? They had an interest

speaker: SS
words: in the schoolhouse, too.

speaker: ES
words: They just said, "No." And you couldn't force them to keep them. So we

speaker: ES
words: explained to the carpenters what the situation was and they said, "That's

speaker: ES
words: fine. We'll sleep in the. cookhouse." I pulled that right up in front

speaker: ES
words: of our cabin there, so there was just a couple of steps between them.

speaker: ES
words: And so, she cooked for 'em. Stiner was up there, he helped 'em on there.

speaker: ES
words: He stayed with us, and he helped. He slept up in the loft. (Chuckles)

speaker: ES
words: He had to almost crawl to get to bed up there. He slept up there. So

speaker: ES
words: we made out.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think these neighbors thought that these carpenters were just

speaker: SS
words: working people and they shouldn't help them because they were better

speaker: SS
words: than they were?

speaker: ES
words: I don't know, and I didn't ask them. I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: How do you think that community up there compared to Park? Were they

speaker: SS
words: similar?

speaker: ES
words: Yes. We had about three different nationalities there for neighbors.

speaker: ES
words: There was two English families and there was one— oh, what were they?-

speaker: ES
words: but they all spoke English, because we were United States people and

speaker: ES
words: there was one Scotch family. And, let's see- there was two English fami

speaker: ES
words: lies, yeah, from different parts England. And two of them was on the

speaker: ES
words: board. The two of them and me, and we had another one for clerk. But

speaker: ES
words: we got along real well. It was a good neighborhood. We got along real

speaker: ES
words: well. Exchanged work and things. In a new neighborhood like that people

speaker: ES
words: need each other, you know. So they decide to get along together, a lot

speaker: ES
words: better than an old- new neighborhood might do. So, we exchanged work

speaker: ES
words: and visited together and run the school and had our Christmas doings and

speaker: ES
words: things. We never had any trouble.

speaker: SS
words: So you would say that it was really a lot like Park would be?

speaker: ES
words: Yes. It was. Very much like that. There was one family that they

speaker: ES
words: were not quite as honest as the others, but we didn't have any trouble

speaker: ES
words: with them, because I made it understood with him myself the first fall

speaker: ES
words: I was there. I rode with him to town before I had bought any horses,

speaker: ES
words: and he started talking about the neighborhood there and he said once, he

speaker: ES
words: said, "You want to keep your house locked when you leave anywhere." His

speaker: ES
words: name was Ben. I says, "Ben, I'll never lock my doors against my neigh

speaker: ES
words: bors. But," I said, "I keep pretty good track of what I've got. And

speaker: ES
words: wherever I find that, if it's missing," I said, "either that man or I'll

speaker: ES
words: get the damndest licking you ever heard tell of." By golly, he believed

speaker: ES
words: it. (Chuckles) We got along fine! We joined land, even, you know. That

speaker: ES
words: post that I had was right up to his place.

speaker: SS
words: You think some of the other neighbors might have had trouble with him?

speaker: ES
words: No. They steered clear of him. Didn't have much to do with them, but I

speaker: ES
words: was next door to him, you know- I mean, we were a mile apart, our houses,

speaker: ES
words: but I joined land with him.

speaker: ES
words: — town. And it was a good little town.

speaker: SS
words: How far from Edmonton?

speaker: ES
words: It's about a hundred and twenty some odd miles. I am not sure of the

speaker: ES
words: exact miles. Well, of course, they have different roads now, so there

speaker: ES
words: are more direct roads into it. So we didn't have to go to Edmonton for

speaker: ES
words: anything much.

speaker: SS
words: It sounds as though it was a lot rougher to make a living up there than

speaker: SS
words: down here.

speaker: ES
words: It was because you couldn't have the variety of crops, you know. You

speaker: ES
words: couldn't have an orchard, and only the hardiest things for garden. You

speaker: ES
words: couldn't even raise alfalfa there.

speaker: SS
words: Really?

speaker: ES
words: No. No, just grain.

speaker: SS
words: Was there a lot of turnover of the people that lived there? A lot of

speaker: SS
words: people selling out?

speaker: ES
words: Not during the time we were there. We had almost the same neighbors

speaker: ES
words: when we left there, they were pretty much the same. You see, they were

speaker: ES
words: mostly homesteaders and that country hadn't been settled long when we

speaker: ES
words: came, so we were all new beginners, pretty much. Some were a few years

speaker: ES
words: ahead of us, but they were still breaking up land.

speaker: SS
words: When you left, where did you come? Did you come back to Park?

speaker: ES
words: We came back to Park, Idaho. We sold our land on crop payments, and of

speaker: ES
words: course, we had to wait a while to try and get started here. And at that

speaker: ES
words: time, why, the old house at Park was vacant. So I contacted my brother

speaker: ES
words: and asked him if I could move in, and he says, "Sure, you move right in."

speaker: ES
words: And we stayed there a while and then began to^move around to see what

speaker: ES
words: could be done. And thefl began to have crop failures again after we left,

speaker: ES
words: you know. One year, one of the buyers there, he- they were honest people

speaker: ES
words: but when they didn't get a crop they couldn't pay. And he was so near

speaker: ES
words: broke one year that all he could send me was thirty-five dollars. The

speaker: ES
words: interest alone at that time would have been $156. But he paid out every

speaker: ES
words: cent he could, that is, my share of the crop. They were selling wheat

speaker: ES
words: one year there for twenty-seven cents a bushel, so you can imagine. I

speaker: ES
words: don't know what they had to live on. There was just he and his wife on

speaker: ES
words: this plot.

speaker: SS
words: Was that land mostly prairie? Or was it timber?

speaker: ES
words: No, it was kind of spotted. There was patches of poplar and willow and

speaker: ES
words: prairie. If you were in a plane you could have looked down on it as just

speaker: ES
words: like a spotted cow OV* something. So, to break up land to amount to any

speaker: ES
words: thing, you had to clear those patches of poplar and willow and that

speaker: ES
words: stuff.

speaker: SS
words: So when you went back to Park, did you just have to wait until you could

speaker: SS
words: any farming?

speaker: ES
words: I rented a place there for a year or two while we were waiting to get

speaker: ES
words: some money to go ahead with, and we didn't get much up there, then I

speaker: ES
words: finally started trucking. I bought a truck and started hauling cedar

speaker: ES
words: poles.

speaker: SS
words: Had Park changed very much between the time you left and time you came

speaker: SS
words: back? Had many of the old-timers gone?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. Most of 'em were gone. There were, see, about three- There was

speaker: ES
words: one outfit that bought several places in there, you know. And what we

speaker: ES
words: called the Noland place, that was where this widower that married a se

speaker: ES
words: cond time; he had the best place in the valley. He was one of the first

speaker: ES
words: ones. And so, when he sold there was new people came and then there was

speaker: ES
words: another outfit came and bought that again, and they bought some other

speaker: ES
words: homesteaders around there, so at one time, Park, most of that was owned

speaker: ES
words: by two different outfits. There were still one or two in there but not

speaker: ES
words: of the ones that were there when we lived there.

speaker: SS
words: When you came back, was there still a community there where people got

speaker: SS
words: together like they had in the older days?

speaker: ES
words: No, there wasn't enough of them hardly, you know. Let's see- there wasdid

speaker: ES
words: you know Charley fiieS^yo**??

speaker: SS
words: Oh, yes, I talked to him.

speaker: ES
words: Well, he was the oldest one in there and the only one left in there when

speaker: ES
words: we came back from Canada. One of my brothers was still there, but they

speaker: ES
words: moved out of there shortly after. And then there was— you may know Nor

speaker: ES
words: man Enger?

speaker: SS
words: I know who he is.

speaker: ES
words: He was born over at Park. And they were still there, but they moved out

speaker: ES
words: of there, too.

speaker: SS
words: Were there still any getting-togethers and that sort of thing with the

speaker: SS
words: new people?

speaker: ES
words: No, not much. We were in there only about two years, I think it was.

speaker: ES
words: We moved out. No, there was just a few and they were new to us, you

speaker: ES
words: know, and so there wasn't much going on. And then they consolidated

speaker: ES
words: with the Deary school district, you know, so there was no school in there.

speaker: SS
words: Did many of those people leave when they sold the timber to Potlatch?

speaker: ES
words: Some of 'em did. There was some that had mostly timber, you know. So

speaker: ES
words: they didn't have much to stay there for, so some of them moved out. But

speaker: ES
words: we moved before that road was built in there so we weren't in contact

speaker: ES
words: with them much from then on.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder if they got what the timber was worth from Potlatch?

speaker: ES
words: No. No, they would sell for— I knew of places sold for around $1,500;

speaker: ES
words: good timber on it. Potlatch was pretty shrewd about it. They'd send

speaker: ES
words: private men in there for them, you see, to buy the place. They'd come

speaker: ES
words: in and say, "I would buy this place."

speaker: SS
words: Then they'd sell and—

speaker: ES
words: But it was finally found out so that they could get a little more.

speaker: SS
words: But it did happen to some people at Park. They sold thinking they were

speaker: SS
words: selling they were selling to a private person.

speaker: ES
words: Yes, they didn't know who they were selling to, you might say. But there

speaker: ES
words: wasn't much of that, and I don't know much about that because we moved

speaker: ES
words: away before there was much of that done. I know one of our neighbors,

speaker: ES
words: he had a timber claim, you know you could file on that, quite a ways back

speaker: ES
words: in over towards what's called the Three Bear Country, and he got a ittle

speaker: ES
words: out of it, but I don't know exactly how much, but he didn't get much.

speaker: SS
words: Did you know Joe Wells? At Deary?

speaker: ES
words: Oh, yeah. Up by Deary there. Yeah. That is I knew about him and

speaker: ES
words: when we lived in Deary, when we put in that water system, one of the

speaker: ES
words: Wells- what was his name? Chuck Wells?

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: ES
words: He worked in there, quite a while. They lived just east of there. And

speaker: ES
words: they had a daughter, was a daughter and a son went to school . Viola

speaker: ES
words: and my kids went to school there in Deary. So they were chums. Yeah.

speaker: ES
words: They were decent people.

speaker: SS
words: When you left Park then, was that the move to Deary? Or to Helmer? Or

speaker: SS
words: where?

speaker: ES
words: We stayed in Helmer one summer; I don't know just how long we stayed

speaker: ES
words: there now, it wasn't very long. Then we moved to Spring Valley. I rent

speaker: ES
words: ed a house there. That was where Floyd started to school, next to Violay

speaker: ES
words: we were close to the schoolhouse there. And I did a little hauling around

speaker: ES
words: And I kept waiting for some money, you know, I didn't get anything from

speaker: ES
